implausible_knowledge: “Pop Rock Desert was a great idea”
error: Rebecca is going to think of Pop Rock Desert, and it will be a great idea.
erroneous_event: Then she tries a better plan: Can she come play in Rebecca’s room? She’ll bring the cupcake-and-ice-cream sticker book, the Jeep, and they can drive Barbie and Ken on the bed, pretend they’re on an adventure, and discover gold using Rebecca’s birthday bead necklace. <error>Rebecca is going to think of Pop Rock Desert, and it will be a great idea.</error> Just let her know yes or no.
story:
A little sister writes Rebecca to give her the BEST reason to give Maliboo Barbie back: she needs her for their adventures. Rebecca’s Ken may match, but Maliboo Barbie is the only one who fits in the Barbie Jeep, and Rebecca always makes Barbie do gross stuff like kiss Ken, which is a big no because boys have cooties and maybe Barbie doesn’t even want to. The narrator promised Maliboo Barbie they’d go to the top of Candy Mountain every day—pinky promise, which you can’t break. Rebecca can come too: they can ride on Pegasus, fly over Gumdrop Rainbow, eat gumdrops for six straight hours, then visit Soda Pop Lake, which changes to any flavor you want. Cherry is Maliboo Barbie’s favorite. Today they must also see the gummy teddy bears, as big as real bears, before hibernation starts and they hide for a very long time. Maliboo Barbie drives the boat at Bubblebath Waterfall, helps when they run across Gummy Worm Jungle (because worms are scary), and is champion at Catch the Leaves—the game where they try to catch falling leaves. At school, all the other Barbies are gross and bald, so Maliboo Barbie is her BEST friend and the only one who fits everywhere she needs to go.

She promises to share: Rebecca can ride her super cool pink bike with sparkle stars and four wheels so she won’t fall, use the smelly markers for coloring, and even borrow the special Cup of Gold for a whole week. She says she loves Rebecca from the bottom of her heart and is asking nicely because she doesn’t want to hurt feelings or involve Mom. Mom already said she’s tired of fixing their problems and they need to solve them themselves; last time, vacuuming was so heavy she sucked up lots of favorite sparkle ponytails and is still sad. If she has to tell, she worries Mom might take Maliboo Barbie away, like Sara’s mom took her dollhouse when she couldn’t get along with her sister. Then Barbie wouldn’t be kissing Ken or going to Candy Mountain or Soda Pop Lake—she’d just stare at the inside of Mom’s closet, which would make Barbie cry and make her cry too. So please be nice and give her back.

Time crawls. After what feels like two hours (even if Rebecca says it’s been only twenty minutes), she writes again: please return Maliboo Barbie now; if Barbie isn’t back in five minutes, she’ll have to tell Mom, which neither of them wants.

Then she tries a better plan: Can she come play in Rebecca’s room? She’ll bring the cupcake-and-ice-cream sticker book, the Jeep, and they can drive Barbie and Ken on the bed, pretend they’re on an adventure, and discover gold using Rebecca’s birthday bead necklace. Rebecca is going to think of Pop Rock Desert, and it will be a great idea. Just let her know yes or no.

Finally, relief: she writes a thank-you for letting her play yesterday—it was soooo fun. Maliboo Barbie says she wants more adventures with Ken, so tell her when Ken is ready. Pop Rock Desert was a great idea; maybe they can use real Pop Rocks next time, though they remember Mom’s rule about no food in the room after the spaghetti-in-bed incident. P.S. she left the sticker book under the letter and saved some pages for Rebecca. She loves her.
